content.text: Despite these achievements in air quality management, China is facing both challenges and opportunities to realize "beautiful China" targets and to absolutely decouple emissions of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and air pollutants from economic growth in the ecological civilization era. On the one hand,  $\mathrm{O}_{3}$  pollution is emerging and could be more challenging in future. On the other hand, the coupling of climate mitigation and air pollution control exhibits significant co-benefits that can lead to a win-win opportunity. An integrated management framework linking energy, environment, health, and climate units should be established for the synergistic mitigation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}, \mathrm{O}_{3}$ , and GHGs. Such a framework is expected to consist of three core components: a precise and dynamic system to reproduce the condition of the regional and global atmospheric environment; a coordinated and highly efficient treatment system to simultaneously mitigate the emissions of air pollutants and GHGs; and a comprehensive decision-supporting platform to form a synergistic mitigation pathway and optimal technical combination. Ultimately, a multi-objective and multi-benefit roadmap is required in order for China to attain air quality standards domestically and to fulfill international agreements concerning GHGs, and so forth.

content.text: In the short and medium term, air pollution controls are expected to act as a leading driver in the synergistic path for realization of these co-benefits. The Three-Year Action Plan for winning the "blue sky defense battle" (2018-2020) explicitly emphasizes the co-benefits between air pollution control and climate change mitigation. In particular, it introduces more stringent measures to limit coal use and encourage clean transportation in China, which will be clearly beneficial for reducing  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions in the related sectors. In addition, the 2035 "beautiful China" targets for air quality (e.g., to decrease annual  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  to below  $35\mu \mathrm{g}\cdot \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) are expected to impose stricter requirements for de-carbonization of the energy system in China in comparison with the climate targets that were committed to in China's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) [75]. In the long term, the WHO air quality guidelines (AQGs) are considered to be the ultimate objective for air quality improvements in China during the process of developing ecological civilization through 2050.

content.text: At the same time, realizing the global  $1.5^{\circ}C / 2^{\circ}C$  climate target will require China to significantly reduce its GHG emissions by 2050 and beyond, as indicated in a large body of scenario analysis [76-78]. Thus, both climate mitigation and air pollution control will become increasingly challenging in the long term. It is clear that a dynamic understanding of the synergistic effects between air pollution control and climate mitigation in China at different stages is critical for the country to follow a cost-effective pathway toward sustainable development and ecological civilization.

content.text: Our study estimated the changes in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in China at a fine-resolution (1 km) during 2014-2020, identifying the measure with greatest environmental and health co-benefits in ACP, and demonstrating the potential of carbon mitigation measures for air quality improvement and public health enhancement. Results showed that (1) the average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in China decreased by  $33.65\%$  ( $13.41~\mu \mathrm{g}~\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) from 2014 to 2020, accompanied by a decrease in total attributed deaths of 0.23 million (95 % CI: 0.22-0.27), indicating the great benefits of China ACP for human health and environment. However, the premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China still reached 1.12 million (95 % CI: 0.79-1.56) in 2020. (2) Among China ACP measures, clean production and energy consumption control made the largest contribution to air quality improvements and health burden mitigation during the past seven years. However, the effectiveness of the two ACP measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. Meanwhile, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic spatially. (3) The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  has become one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase, confirming the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures.

content.text: The Global Burden of Disease Study (GBD) 2015 identified ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure as a significant risk factor in the disease burden in China and as responsible for 1.1 million deaths in that year [16]. Based on the GBD project, the World Bank Group estimated that the premature mortality costs due to air pollution (including indoor exposure) were close to  $10\%$  of the GDP equivalent in 2013—significantly higher than those of developed countries in Europe and North America [17]. Undoubtedly, given the scale of the impacts of air pollution on public health, improvement of air quality has become a central domain in developing ecological civilization in China.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its Challenges and Opportunities in the Ecological Civilization Era", "md_filename": "Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its.md", "paragraph_index": 27, "content_length": 681, "chunk_type": "text"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: The 2015 Global Burden of Disease Study attributed 1.1 million deaths to $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ in China, highlighting the crucial need to prioritize air quality improvement.
evaluation.reason: Reinforces the serious health and economic costs of poor air quality in China.

content.text: The inclusion of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  as a criteria pollutant in the 2012 amendment of NAAQS marked the strategic and philosophical evolvement of air pollution control in China. Ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration had become a more transparent indicator for city residents in China than other air pollutants, as the public began to gradually understand the relationship between  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution, atmospheric visibility, and health impact. The major objectives of the Air Pollution Prevention and Control Action Plan 2013-2017 (Action Plan, hereinafter) launched by the State Council were to improve air quality and reduce heavy pollution days nationwide. Unlike the previous TCZ policy, which was proposed by the SEPA and then approved by the State Council, the formulation of the Action Plan was directly led and promoted by the State Council for the first time in the area of air pollution control. The higher level of authority for the Action Plan reflected the fact that combating  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution would require more coordinated efforts at the national and regional levels than ever before. Three developed regions—the BTH, Yangtze River Delta (YRD), and Pearl River Delta (PRD)—had more stringent targets: to reduce average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations by  $25\%$ ,  $20\%$ , and  $15\%$ , respectively, during 2013-2017. Although variation in the meteorological conditions from 2013 to 2017 slightly favored reducing  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in the BTH region, emission reductions were the dominating factor for the decline of ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration and the associated health benefits [30]. Beijing set a goal that the annual average concentration in 2017 should be below  $60~\mu \mathrm{g} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , which required a reduction within five years that was greater than  $30\%$  of the 2013 level ( $89~\mu \mathrm{g} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ).

evaluation.extracted_insight: The Air Pollution Prevention and Control Action Plan (2013-2017) achieved significant reductions in $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ concentrations in China's most polluted areas through comprehensive measures.
evaluation.reason: Historical analysis of measures and their regional effects is valuable as contextual material.

content.text: The increase in population size and economic activity has remarkably increased the demand for fossil fuels in China, posing a severe threat to the environment. However, the recently implemented coal-based energy transition has shifted the energy structure from polluting energies to environment-friendly energies, and the subsequent policies created a great revolution in energy development and environment protection. Nevertheless, the energy distribution and air pollution in China are characterized by regionality, thus, targeting the efficiency of these national control policies, scholars mainly focus on regions with high-polluting and energy-intensive industries, such as Shanxi province and the BJH region; or target a specific policy, such as CTG and CTE policy. Differently, this study revealed the air quality improvement during the implementation of CCTPs at the national level. Clarifying the internal mechanism of these policies is significant when revising and implementing the following policies to achieve sustainable development. The quantitative assessment provided significant results: i) After implementing CCTPs, the Chinese mainland experienced air quality improvement, with the reduction of AQI averaging  $18.82\%$ ; ii) An identical reduction of air pollution was observed in 2018, indicating a 2-year time lag of the related control policies; iii) There were positive associations between emission reduction promoting, deindustrialization and air quality improvement, suggesting two promising ways to realize sustainable energy development. Through assessing the air quality changes before and after the implementation of CCTPs, it is expected to provide a scientific basis for policymakers and stakeholders to forge consensus on the establishment and enforcement of clean coal policies.

content.text: transformed into a new model: In contrast to its initial focus on control actions for a single pollutant or in a single sector, air quality management has now been redesigned to mitigate multiple precursors from multiple sectors through extensive coordination at the regional or national level. More recently, China has launched a series of movements including the short-term "blue sky defense battle" between 2018 and 2020, and the long-term "beautiful China" targets through 2035. The latter require fundamental improvements in air quality in China; in particular, they require all cities in China to achieve an annual concentration of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  below  $35\mu \mathrm{g}\cdot \mathrm{m}^{-3}$  (the Interim Target-1 level recommended by the World Health Organization (WHO)) by 2035. To realize this goal, in-depth adjustment of the industrial structure and de-carbonization of energy systems will be critical, in addition to more stringent end-of-pipe control measures.

content.text: Air pollution is one of the greatest environmental problems the world faces today, leading to significant undesirable effects. Being the greatest environmental risk to health, air pollution threatens human health by increasing the morbidity and mortality of respiratory disease, heart disease, lung cancer, diabetes, etc. [1-3], contributing to estimated 7 million premature deaths each year [4]. Although typical air pollutants in China have decreased significantly in recent years, air pollution in China remains inferior to that experienced on average globally. According to the latest 2018 reports released by the IQAir [5] and Greenpeace [6], China ranks third in  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions and twelfth in annual average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations globally.

args.query_intent: methods to reduce uncertainty in quantifying air pollution from biomass burning and transportation in China, including MEIC and satellite-based techniques

orchestrator_plan.reason: To address specific uncertainties in quantifying air pollution sources like biomass burning and transportation, and explore the application of advanced tools like the MEIC model and satellite imaging (e.g., VIIRS).
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
### Summary of Retrieval Process

The root goal of the investigation was to determine how to accurately assess the sources, mitigation effectiveness, and environmental health impacts of air pollution in China. This inquiry required a deep dive into advanced methods and tools for quantifying air pollution, particularly focusing on uncertainties related to emissions from biomass burning and transportation. The strategic direction aimed at combining advanced models, like the Multi-resolution Emission Inventory for China (MEIC), with satellite-based tools, such as those utilizing data from the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS).

The retrieval strategy employed was **semantic search**, which focused on identifying methods to reduce the uncertainty in quantifying air pollution. The search utilized the specific query: _"methods to reduce uncertainty in quantifying air pollution from biomass burning and transportation in China, including MEIC and satellite-based techniques"_, and retrieved 20 results. Each of these results was evaluated for relevance, credibility, and insight contribution.

---

### Highlights from Retrieval Outcomes

1. **Relevance and Quality Assessment**:
   - Out of the 20 results retrieved, a mix of high- and low-quality documents was included. High-quality sources were retained for their pertinence and insight value, while less relevant items, such as those containing meta-information (e.g., researcher affiliations without substantive content), were pruned.
   - Retained results had mostly moderate-to-high scores for relevance and credibility (e.g., around 7 to 8 on an unspecified scale).

2. **Key Findings**:
   - **Emissions Estimation from Biomass Burning**:
     One highlighted document detailed methods for estimating biomass burning emissions in China using MODIS fire radiative energy data. This study spans a longer period (2003–2017) and provides a baseline for understanding remote sensing technologies in emissions estimation. However, it also acknowledges technical uncertainties, particularly in overestimating fire counts when relying solely on satellite data.
   - **Uncertainty in Bottom-Up Emission Inventories**:
     Another retained document addressed uncertainties in anthropogenic atmospheric pollutant inventories. Specifically, it highlighted how emission quantification through bottom-up approaches (e.g., using MEIC) can vary due to different data inputs and methodological assumptions.
   - **Remote Sensing Insights**:
     Remote sensing applications were compared across different methodologies. Among observed limitations were the inaccuracies in measuring emission counts directly from satellite fire data, with specific weaknesses like over-reliance on fire count metrics leading to overestimation.
   - **International Collaboration to Reduce Uncertainty**:
     Phase III of MICS-Asia, an interregional scientific effort, was referenced as a major initiative aimed at improving emission inventory accuracy across Asia. This initiative emphasizes model intercomparison and the development of harmonized emission datasets, highlighting an important collaborative effort for better air pollution modeling in the region.

3. **Limitations and Gaps**:
   - Some of the documents included general descriptions of methodologies without offering actionable insights into mitigation effectiveness or environmental health impacts.
   - One retrieved item referenced Non-Methane Volatile Organic Compound (NMVOC) impacts but lacked detailed actionable information or specific applications for improving air quality assessments.
   - Duplicate content was observed in at least one instance, indicating potential inefficiencies in retrieval refinement.

---

### Retrieval Quality Commentary

1. **Relevance**: The retrieved documents were moderately relevant to the overarching goal, with key papers addressing the uncertainties in emission quantification for biomass burning and anthropogenic emissions. However, a number of low-relevance documents (e.g., meta-content) reduced overall effectiveness.

2. **Accuracy**: Credibility of the retained results appeared strong, as the documents were derived from authoritative sources discussing advanced methodologies. Still, there were gaps in directly linking these methods to concrete environmental health outcomes.

3. **Authority**: The included works referenced key tools like MEIC and satellite-based techniques and involved high-impact initiatives like MICS-Asia, suggesting a focus on established, credible research efforts.

4. **Completeness**: While the search uncovered diverse aspects of emission quantification (e.g., remote sensing, bottom-up inventories), it fell short of addressing mitigation effectiveness and environmental health impacts in depth. There was little focus on specific policy applications or subsidiary effects of air pollution control strategies.

---

### Observations and Recommendations

Although the retrieval process produced valuable insights into quantification challenges, it left substantial gaps in addressing the full scope of the root goal, particularly mitigation effectiveness and environmental health consequences. The analysis shows that while the search strategy was effective at capturing technical uncertainties and methodological comparisons, future iterations should:

- Expand queries to explicitly connect emission quantification with mitigation outcomes.
- Target evidence related to policy-driven applications of these methodologies.
- Seek more detailed perspectives on environmental health impacts beyond technical emissions data.

This approach would yield a more holistic view of air pollution challenges and better align with the multifaceted intent of assessing environmental and health impacts in China.
orchestrator_plan.hyde_hypothetical_paragraph_full_text: Reducing uncertainty in quantifying air pollution from biomass burning and transportation in China requires integrating multiple data sources and advanced modeling approaches. The Multi-resolution Emission Inventory for China (MEIC) provides a robust bottom-up framework for estimating emissions with high spatial and temporal resolution, but uncertainties can be minimized by incorporating region-specific activity data and emission factors. Satellite-based remote sensing techniques, such as those using TROPOMI and MODIS instruments, offer top-down constraints by providing real-time observations of atmospheric pollutants like NO₂, CO, and aerosols. Combining MEIC with satellite-derived data through data assimilation techniques or inverse modeling can improve the accuracy of emission estimates by reconciling discrepancies between modeled and observed pollutant concentrations. Additionally, leveraging ground-based monitoring networks and chemical transport models, such as WRF-Chem, can further refine spatial and temporal emission patterns, enhancing the reliability of air quality assessments.

content.text: Based on our review of the spatial proxies used in bottom-up emission inventories for China, it can be concluded that such spatial proxies are empirical and may have introduced considerable uncertainties into the spatial distributions of emissions. Studies on fossil fuel  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  inventories have made tremendous efforts to improve the spatial distribution of emissions based on satellite-derived nighttime light (Oda et al., 2011), fuel sales and traffic data (McDonald et al., 2014), multivariate regressions (Wang et al., 2013; Gately et al., 2015), or combined fossil fuel data assimilation systems (Rayner et al., 2010; Asefi-Najafabady et al., 2014), shedding new light on ways to improve the spatial distribution of air pollutant inventories.

content.text: Besides national ones, increasing attentions have been paid on regional/local inventories, motivated mainly by the urgent needs for haze pollution mitigation in specific regions across the country (Zheng et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2010; Fu et al., 2013; Zhao et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2017). Zhao et al. (2015) incorporated detailed information on individual plants and developed a city scale emission inventory for Nanjing in eastern China. The emission estimates, compared to the downscaled national inventory, were more consistent with the "top-down" constraints obtained from ground/ satellite observation. Better model performances could also be achieved when the local inventory was used in high-resolution CTM (Zhou et al., 2017). To further understand the discrepancies between observation and CTM simulation, quantifying the uncertainties of emissions at small spatial scales gets essential. Nevertheless, very limited studies have quantified the uncertainty of emission inventory at local scale, except for given species (e.g., biogenic volatile organic compounds and  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  in Pearl River Delta by Zheng et al. (2010) and (2012), respectively) or single source type (e.g., biomass burning by He et al. (2011), on-road vehicles by Wang et al. (2008), and ships by Li et al. (2016)). Moreover, the methods and results of uncertainty analyses could potentially vary a lot for national and local inventories based on different data sources and depth of details. Current available studies, however, simply followed the method for national inventory and seldom conducted careful analysis on individual emission sources. To current knowledge of data and methods for multi-scale inventory development, the uncertainty of emission estimation at local scale, and its difference between local and national inventories remain unclear.

content.text: Biomass burning exerts substantial influences on air quality and climate, which in turn to further aggravate air quality. The biomass burning emissions in particular of the agricultural burning may suffer large uncertainties which limits the understanding of their impact on air quality. Based on an improved emission inventory of the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS) relative to commonly used Global Fire Emissions Database (GFED), we thoroughly evaluate the impact of biomass burning on air quality and climate during the episodes of November 2017 in Northeast China which is rich in agriculture burning. The results first indicate substantial underestimates in simulated  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations without the inclusion of biomass burning emission inventory, based on a regional air quality model Weather Research and Forecasting model and Community Multiscale Air Quality model (WRF-CMAQ). The addition of biomass burning emissions from GFED then reduces the bias to a certain extent, which is further reduced by replacing the agricultural fires data in GFED with VIIRS. Numerical sensitivity experiments show that based on the improved emission inventory, the contribution of biomass burning emissions to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in Northeast China reaches  $32\%$ , contrasting to  $15\%$  based on GFED, during the episode from November 1 to 7, 2017. Aerosol direct radiative effects from biomass burning are finally elucidated, which not only reduce downward surface shortwave radiation and planetary boundary layer height, but also affect the vertical distribution of air temperature, wind speed and relative humidity, favorable to the accumulation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . During November 1-7, 2017, the mean daily  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  enhancement due to aerosol radiative effects from VIIRS_G is  $16\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , a few times higher than that of  $2.8\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  from GFED. The study stresses the critical role of biomass burning, particularly of small fires easily missed in the traditional low-resolution satellite products, on air quality.

content.text: To this end, we first make a thorough evaluation of the air quality model in reproducing severe pollution events in Northeast China, through the application of the traditional and higher-resolution emission inventory, with the hypothesis that the higher-resolution emission inventory such as VIIRS may strengthen the capability of numerical models in reproducing the severe air pollution events. What is followed is to examine the contribution of biomass burning emissions on air quality in Northeast China based on the improved emission inventory, as well as the elucidation of aerosol radiative effects of biomass burning on aggravating the air quality. Considering the widespread agricultural burning worldwide, the study highlights the importance of re-examining the impact of agricultural burning on air quality based on more accurate emission inventory in broader areas.

content.text: Abstract. Spatial proxies used in bottom-up emission inventories to derive the spatial distributions of emissions are usually empirical and involve additional levels of uncertainty. Although uncertainties in current emission inventories have been discussed extensively, uncertainties resulting from improper spatial proxies have rarely been evaluated. In this work, we investigate the impact of spatial proxies on the representation of gridded emissions by comparing six gridded  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission datasets over China developed from the same magnitude of emissions and different spatial proxies. GEOS-Chem-modeled tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns simulated from different gridded emission inventories are compared with satellite-based columns. The results show that differences between modeled and satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns are sensitive to the spatial proxies used in the gridded emission inventories. The total population density is less suitable for allocating  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions than nighttime light data because population density tends to allocate more emissions to rural areas. Determining the exact locations of large emission sources could significantly strengthen the correlation between modeled and observed  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns. Using vehicle population and an updated road network for the on-road transport sector could substantially en

content.text: The spatial proxies used when developing gridded emission inventories are empirical and can introduce uncertainties in bottom-up emission inventories. This issue has rarely been evaluated. In this work, we evaluate the effects of spatial proxies on the representations of spatial distributions of emissions using an integrated framework of bottom-up emission inventories, a chemical transport model, and satellite observations. We first develop six sets of gridded  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions for China using the same magnitude of emissions from the MEIC and different spatial proxies. The spatial proxies considered in this study include the following: total population, nighttime lights, locations of power plants, IGDP, vehicle populations, and two different road network datasets. The nested-grid GEOS-Chem model is then used to simulate tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns using the six gridded emissions, and modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns are compared to satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns derived from OMI data.

content.text: concern (Chen et al., 2017a). Early studies used provincial statistical data to estimate biomass burning emissions. This method required many parameters that depend on local environment or agricultural practices and could vary greatly in different research studies, leading to significant emission uncertainties (Liu et al., 2015). Studies statistically evaluated fire emissions in China with results of annual  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions of  $68 - 150\mathrm{Tg}$  from crop residue burning (Ni et al., 2015; Huang et al., 2012; Li et al., 2016b) and  $3 - 40\mathrm{Tg}$  from forest fires (Lu et al., 2006; Yan et al., 2006). This approach produced emission estimates at a coarse resolution that cannot be used for detailed analysis of spatiotemporal patterns. Thus, two methods based on remote sensing data has been increasingly used. The first method is based on fire count data provided by active fire products. In this approach, a maximum burned area of  $1\mathrm{km}^2$  is assumed for each fire count detected. Mehmood et al. (2018) calculated the mean emission of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  for the period of 2002-2016 as  $160\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  (with  $24\mathrm{Tg}$  from crop residue burning) by using data derived from the Fire INventory from NCAR version 1.5 (FINNv1.5), which was established by the fire count method (Wiedinmyer et al., 2011). Because the actual area burned for each fire count could vary to a large extent, using fire counts as a proxy for fire-affected area may lead to a large potential error in emission estimates (Song et al., 2009). The other method is based on the burned area products (Song et al., 2010). The estimated emission is a product of burned area  $(\mathrm{km}^2)$ , aboveground biomass density burned in fields  $(\mathrm{kgm}^{-2}$  of dry matter), combustion efficiency (\%), and emission factor  $(\mathrm{gkg}^{-1})$  for each pollutant. Generally, the uncertainty originates from all of the above factors. Moreover, as the average cultivated area of a farming household is very limited in China (around  $10^{4}\mathrm{m}^{2}$ ), each agricultural fire burns within a small extent (Liu et al., 2015). Therefore, the fire count method is likely to overestimate the burned area of crop residue burning, and these fires are not detected efficiently by the available burned area algorithms due to the small areas and intermittency (Song et al., 2009).

content.text: the aerosols emitted by biomass burning strikingly steer the development of the PBL by changing meteorological conditions, which then subsequently induce the accumulation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations. The underlying message delivered in this study indicates an urgent need to re-evaluate how the previously missed biomass burning emissions, particularly of regions with strong agriculture burning, might affect the air quality at present as well as in future. Moreover, considering the potential underestimation in VIIRS, it is encouraged to continue to improve the biomass burning emission inventory, e.g., adding fire FRP data measured by other satellites (Fengyun 4 geostationary satellite and NOAA20 polar orbiting satellite) or fire burned area provided by satellite with high the spatial resolution (e.g. Sentinel-2A), so as to further enhance the capability of numerical models in assessing biomass burning induced air quality issues. In addition, the spatial resolution applied in this study is  $36\mathrm{km}$  by  $36\mathrm{km}$ , and a higher resolution is desired to potentially better quantify the impact of biomass burning on air quality in particular of burning with a strong intensity in a relatively small area.

Although Monte Carlo simulation yields narrowed estimates of uncertainties compared to previous bottom-up emission studies, the results are not always consistent with those derived from satellite observations. The results thus represent an incremental research advance; while the analysis provides current estimates of uncertainty to researchers investigating Chinese and global atmospheric transport and chemistry, it also identifies specific needs in data collection and analysis to improve on them. Strengthened quantification of emissions of the included species and other, closely associated ones – notably  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ , generated largely by the same processes and thus subject to many of the same parameter uncertainties – is essential not only for science but for the design of policies to redress critical atmospheric environmental hazards at local, regional, and global scales.

content.text: The Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) project is currently in phase III. During the previous two phases, studies have been focused on long-range transport and deposition of pollutants, global inflow of pollutants to Asia, model sensitivities to aerosol parameterization, and emissions over Asia (Carmichael et al., 2002, 2008; Han et al., 2008; Hayami et al., 2008; Holloway et al., 2008; Wang et al., 2008). MICS-Asia Phase III aims to conduct further intercomparisons of atmospheric modeling for Asia and analyze the disagreement of model output and relative uncertainties. In this regard, common meteorological fields, emission data, and boundary conditions should be used. One of the key tasks in MICS-Asia Phase III is to develop a reliable Asian emission inventory as common input for model intercomparisons through integration of state-of-the-art knowledge on Asian emissions.

content.text: Most studies regarding the inversion of NMVOC emissions or its impact on  $\mathrm{O}_3$  neglected the uncertainties associated with  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -dependent production or loss of NMVOC oxidation and  $\mathrm{O}_3$ . An iteratively nonlinear joint inversion of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  and NMVOCs using multi-species observations is expected to minimize the uncertainties in their emissions and is well-suited to address the intricate relationship among VOC- $\mathrm{NO}_x$ - $\mathrm{O}_3$ . In this study, we extended the Regional multi-Air Pollutant Assimilation System (RAPAS) upon the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) assimilation algorithm to enhance the optimization of NMVOC emissions over China, utilizing the TROPOspheric Monitoring Instrument (TROPOMI) HCHO retrievals with high spatial coverage and resolution. To more accurately quantify the impact of NMVOC emissions on  $\mathrm{O}_3$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions were simultaneously adjusted using nationwide in-situ  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  observations. Process analysis was subsequently employed to quantify various chemical pathways associated with  $\mathrm{O}_3$  formation and loss. Through a top-down constraint on both emissions, this study aims to offer a more scientific insight into the consequences of optimizing NMVOC emissions on  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and contribute to the development of appropriate emission reduction policies.

args.query_intent: uncertainty analysis in emission inventories

orchestrator_plan.reason: To narrow the focus and pinpoint specific methodologies or techniques addressing uncertainty in emission inventory data, which is crucial for evaluating mitigation strategies in China.
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
The retrieval process sought to address the root goal of understanding how to accurately assess the sources, mitigation effectiveness, and environmental health impacts of air pollution in China. Specifically, the first search iteration focused on a targeted aspect of this challenge: uncertainty analysis in emission inventories. This focus aimed to identify specific methodologies or techniques that could refine or improve the accuracy of emission data, a key step in evaluating both the sources of pollution and the effectiveness of mitigation measures.

To execute the search, the strategy_exact_search method was employed, with parameters that included the intent “uncertainty analysis in emission inventories” and a request for 20 results. The reasoning behind this approach was the need to streamline the search and directly pinpoint resources that address the reliability of emission inventories—an essential component for informing sound environmental policies and gauging the health impacts of air pollution in China.

Despite this focused and deliberate strategy, the retrieval process yielded no results (hit count: 0). This represents a significant gap in the exploration effort, as no relevant insights or data could be retrieved from the attempted search approach. As a result, the process did not advance the root goal of constructing a robust framework for assessing air pollution in China.

### Analysis of Retrieval Outcomes and Dimensions of Quality
The retrieval outcomes were limited entirely by the absence of results. Several potential factors may have contributed to this outcome:

1. **Relevance**: The use of the specific query intent “uncertainty analysis in emission inventories” might have been overly narrow, excluding broader discussions related to emission inventories, uncertainty quantification, or air pollution in general.
2. **Accuracy**: While the intent was clear and focused, it is possible that the exact phrasing did not align with the terminology used in available documents or datasets, leading to no matches.
3. **Authority**: The absence of hits makes it impossible to evaluate whether authoritative and credible sources on the topic were available but overlooked due to phrasing or strategy limitations.
4. **Completeness**: The failure to return results underlines the limitations of the retrieval strategy in this context. A broader query or additional strategies might be necessary to achieve a more complete view of the topic.

### Recommendations for Future Retrieval Iterations
Given the lack of results, several adjustments should be considered to improve subsequent retrieval efforts:

1. **Broaden Query Parameters**: Expanding the scope of the search to include related topics, such as “emission inventories and air pollution in China” or “methods for quantifying uncertainty in environmental data,” may yield more results. This approach could help capture relevant data that does not explicitly use the original phrasing.
   
2. **Experiment with Alternative Strategies**: In addition to exact search techniques, using more exploratory or semantic search methods could facilitate the identification of closely related materials. For instance, a query that leverages natural language processing might recognize connections between different terminologies or concepts.

3. **Incorporate Secondary Topics**: If no results are directly available for uncertainty in emission inventories, the search might pivot to adjacent areas, such as analyzing case studies of air quality mitigation in China or reviewing global best practices for emission inventory accuracy.

4. **Leverage Specific Databases**: Directing the search to specialized environmental research databases, government reports, or academic publications on air pollution may increase the probability of finding high-quality, relevant data.

### Conclusion
The first iteration to address the root goal—understanding air pollution in China through analyses of sources, mitigation, and impacts—was not successful in retrieving actionable insights. While the query intent was precise and fit the context of uncertainty analysis in emission inventories, it may have been too restrictive to yield results. Future cycles should adjust parameters to ensure broader coverage, while exploring alternate search strategies and data sources to improve relevance and completeness.

args.query_intent: What deep learning models have been used to forecast air pollution trends and assess health outcomes of China's Air Clean Plan over time?

orchestrator_plan.reason: This follow-up refines the focus on specific algorithms or models related to forecasting and evaluating health outcomes under China's Air Clean Plan, addressing the need for insights into deep learning approaches.
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
To answer the root goal of assessing the sources, mitigation effectiveness, and environmental health impacts of air pollution in China, the retrieval process focused on identifying deep learning models applied to forecast air pollution trends and evaluate outcomes of China's Air Clean Plan. The strategic approach involved a semantic search using the query, “What deep learning models have been used to forecast air pollution trends and assess health outcomes of China's Air Clean Plan over time?” The system aimed to gather 20 highly relevant results to provide insights into algorithmic approaches and their effectiveness in air pollution assessment.

### Strategy Execution and Retrieval Outcomes

The semantic search retrieved 20 hits, with evaluation criteria emphasizing relevance and credibility. Among the results, samples highlighted deep learning’s advantages in air pollution prediction and health impact assessment. Key methods included satellite-based PM2.5 data, CNN-LSTM models, and the integration of high-dimensional spatiotemporal inputs into frameworks like TensorFlow/Keras. These approaches were particularly effective in accommodating complex features such as meteorological variables, atmospheric dispersion conditions, and regional nuances.

#### Sample Results Breakdown:
1. **Result 1** discussed the superiority of deep learning methods compared to traditional empirical techniques. These models excel in handling nonlinear relationships between air pollution variables, a critical factor for accurate forecasting.
   
2. **Result 2** highlighted the encoding of spatiotemporal dependencies using meteorological variables such as temperature and wind power. These factors significantly enhance air quality prediction models.

3. **Result 3** focused on the CNN-LSTM deep learning architecture. This hybrid model, combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, tailored for China’s provincial cities, demonstrated strong performance with average RMSE values of 22.06 for Air Quality Index (AQI) prediction—a major advancement in high-accuracy forecasting.

Collectively, the search provided strong thematic alignment with the query intent, extracting robust insights into forecasting methodologies and their implications for environmental policies.

---

### Key Insights on Air Pollution Analysis in China

#### 1. **Role of Satellite-Based PM2.5 Data**
Satellite data played a pivotal role in improving the granularity and accuracy of spatiotemporal air pollution analysis in China. Models utilizing this data achieved remarkably low RMSE values (4.82 μg/m³), underscoring their utility in assessing air quality over multiple regions. This comprehensive spatial analysis enabled more effective monitoring of pollution levels and mitigation outcomes.

#### 2. **Deep Learning Models for Enhanced Prediction**
Variants of the CNN-LSTM architecture emerged as highly effective tools for forecasting air pollution. By capturing spatiotemporal dependencies—a key challenge in environmental modeling—these models optimized multi-dimensional input structures for time-series predictions. Another innovation in this domain was the combination of compressed fine-tuning processes with meteorological data, which bolstered the accuracy and reliability of prediction outputs.

#### 3. **Impact of the Air Clean Plan on Pollution Reduction**
China’s Air Clean Plan (2014-2020) demonstrated substantial mitigation achievements. The initiative led to a 33.65% reduction in PM2.5 levels nationwide, which translated into significant health benefits, such as lowered mortality rates from respiratory and cardiovascular illnesses. However, regional disparities in pollution levels persisted, highlighting the need for equitable policy enforcement and localized interventions.

#### 4. **Integration of Deep Learning for Health Outcome Assessment**
The retrieved studies underscore the potential of TensorFlow/Keras-based frameworks in capturing the intersection of environmental data and public health. Leveraging high-dimensional inputs (e.g., meteorological conditions, pollutant concentrations), these approaches provided holistic analyses of how air quality improvements under the Air Clean Plan translated into measurable health benefits.

---

### Retrieval Insights: Evaluation of Quality Dimensions

The quality of retrieved information was evaluated based on relevance, accuracy, authority, and completeness:

1. **Relevance:** Retrieval aligned closely with the query intent, focusing on deep learning methodologies and their applications to China’s air pollution mitigation assessment. Models such as CNN-LSTM and satellite-based PM2.5 data demonstrated high relevance.

2. **Accuracy:** Specific numerical results (e.g., RMSE values, pollution reduction rates) lend credibility to the findings. The quantification of impacts (33.65% PM2.5 reduction, RMSE values averaging 22.06) was well-supported, strengthening the accuracy dimension.

3. **Authority:** Specific studies cited were tied to authoritative frameworks and widely utilized model architectures like TensorFlow/Keras. This reinforces the reliability of the conclusions.

4. **Completeness:** While regional disparities in pollution levels were acknowledged, the retrieval could benefit from more diverse studies detailing mitigation challenges, as well as health outcome data beyond mortality statistics.

---

### Final Assessment and Implications

This retrieval task effectively synthesized cutting-edge insights into air pollution forecasting and mitigation evaluation in China. The integration of deep learning models (e.g., CNN-LSTM) and satellite-based datasets revealed their high potential to drive effective, scalable environmental policies. Real-world applications, such as the Air Clean Plan’s measurable reductions in PM2.5 levels, were well-linked to health impact analyses. However, future efforts could refine completeness by exploring a broader spectrum of mitigation strategies and outcomes.

In conclusion, the retrieval results provide a strong foundation for understanding how deep learning approaches and environmental interventions have shaped air quality management in China. These tools showcase their ability to address complex challenges in forecasting, environmental health assessment, and spatiotemporal modeling, thereby contributing meaningfully to cleaner air and healthier populations.
orchestrator_plan.hyde_hypothetical_paragraph_full_text: Recent studies have employed deep learning models such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and hybrid architectures combining LSTM with attention mechanisms to forecast air pollution trends and evaluate the health impacts of China's Air Clean Plan. These models leverage spatiotemporal data, including historical air quality indices (AQI), meteorological variables, and satellite-derived aerosol optical depth (AOD), to predict pollutant concentrations and assess temporal changes. Additionally, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have been utilized to capture spatial dependencies across monitoring stations, while integrating epidemiological data to estimate reductions in respiratory and cardiovascular disease burdens attributable to improved air quality. These approaches demonstrate the efficacy of deep learning in quantifying both environmental and public health outcomes of large-scale policy interventions.

content.text: Compared with traditional techniques for forecasting air pollution, namely empirical approaches and simple statistical models, deep learning approaches can handle complex and non-linear relationships among air quality variables which allow them to better capture the pollution episode forecasts. Therefore, deep learning has been identified as a vital tool for air quality modelling and forecasting, which helps in recognizing pollution hot spots (Schmidhuber, 2015; Ong et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017).

content.text: Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/).

content.text: The study obtained the data including a total of 1440 daily air quality and meteorological data (January 22, 2016, to December 31, 2019) from 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland,  $60\%$  of which are chosen as the training set,  $20\%$  as the validation set and  $20\%$  as the test set. After determining the best basic network architecture for the current prediction tasks, models are set up in units of cities, and the training set is used to train the tailored CNN-LSTM models. The accuracy indicators of the model prediction results of 31 cities, namely RMSE values averaging 22.06, models perform well overall, indicating that the applied CNN-LSTM network has good capabilities for AQI prediction (Jin et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2021).

source_args.query_intent: What deep learning models have been used to forecast air pollution trends and assess health outcomes of China's Air Clean Plan over time?

evaluation.extracted_insight: The system architecture for AQI prediction integrates CNN-LSTM models, extending to data preprocessing, training, and result analysis.
evaluation.reason: The methodology's framework provides a strong foundation for technical expansion in air quality forecasting.

content.text: Different from the previous policy effectiveness studies, which focused on a specific region or policy with little to national and industrial overview, the main goal of this study is to systematically and comprehensively measure the air quality variation after the implementation of CCTPs at the national level. Based on China's air quality and meteorological data from 2013 to 2019, deep learning approaches were applied to forecast the air quality in 2020, eliminating the uncertainties that COVID-19 brought about. Then, the spatial autocorrelation analysis was utilized to investigate the temporal and spatial distribution characteristics of air pollution. And applying socioeconomic data, the association between AQI change and socioeconomic factors was calculated to explore the most likely mechanism of CCTPs. Our findings showed that there was a significant air quality improvement after the implementation of CCTPs, indicating that clean coal transformation adapts to the requirements of sustainable development in China. And two main channels to reduce air pollution were excavated, namely industrialization and environmental governance. This is essential to reaching an agreement on clean coal utilization, supporting the formulation of energy policies, and combating climate change.

content.text: China implemented a stringent Air Clean Plan (ACP) since 2013 to address environmental and health risks caused by ambient fine particulate matter  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . However, the policy effectiveness of ACP and co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures to environment and health are still largely unknown. Using satellite-based  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products produced in our previous study, concentration-response functions, and the logarithmic mean Divisia index (LMDI) method, we analyzed the spatiotemporal dynamics of premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and quantitatively estimated the policy benefits of ACP and carbon mitigation measures. We found the annual  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in China decreased by  $33.65\%$ $(13.41\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  from 2014 to 2020, accompanied by a decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths of 0.23 million  $(95\%$  confidence interval (CI): 0.22-0.27), indicating the huge benefits of China ACP for human health and environment. However, there were still 1.12 million  $(95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by the exposure of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in mainland China in 2020. Among all ACP measures, clean production (contributed  $55.98\%$  and  $51.14\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) and energy consumption control (contributed  $32.58\%$  and  $29.54\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) made the largest contribution during the past seven years. Nevertheless, the environmental and health benefits of ACP are not fully synergistic in different regions, and the effectiveness of ACP measures reduced from 2018 to 2020. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  has become one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction since 2018, confirming the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Our study suggests, with the saturation of clean production and source control, more targeted region-specific strategies and synergistic air pollution-carbon mitigation measures are critical to achieving the WHO's Air Quality Guideline target and the UN's Sustainable Development Goal Target in China.

content.text: By investigating the effective national control policies, this study has implications for theoretical research on the effectiveness of energy policies. The most valid deployment and efficiency of energy policies have been hotly debated in the literature. The single regional model is not applicable because it lacks a national perspective, as same as research on an individual policy. This single view is not conducive to allocating governance resources reasonably and establishing regional emission reduction targets and regulations. In addition, regarding uncertainties would be brought about when discussing the air quality change since the outbreak of COVID-19, the introduced deep learning approaches could supply researchers with new insights.

content.text: Clean and efficient use of coal in China entered the implementation stage in 2015, and a series of policies were established to promote the sustainable development of clean coal technologies (CCTs). However, the long-term effects of the clean coal technology policies (CCTPs) have not been evaluated, and the outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) would bring great uncertainty regarding the effectiveness evaluation. Employing deep learning approaches and spatial autocorrelation analysis, the present work intended to explore the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs, and investigate the association between air quality and socioeconomic factors to explore the internal mechanism of air quality improvement. Results showed that after implementing CCTPs, the air quality index (AQI) had an average reduction of  $18.82\%$  and the identical drop in air pollution in 2018 implied a 2-year time lag of CCTPs. Additionally, emission reduction and deindustrialization were explored as two promising ways to improve air quality while promoting energy transformation. The findings indicated that the Chinese government ought to pay more attention to long-term plans; industrial restructuring and environmental protection should be insisted upon to realize sustainable energy development. By providing a perspective on air quality improvement after the policies are implemented, this study can lead practitioners and academia to a comprehensive and objective view of clean coal policies.

content.text: Annual satellite-based  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products covering mainland China from 2014 to 2020 (Fig. S2) were used to investigate the spatiotemporal variation in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and to estimate  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable deaths. This dataset was produced by our previous studies (Wang et al., 2021; 2022) through the combination of satellite-based remote sensing data, site-based  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  observation data, and an improved deep learning model. It has the finest spatial resolution (1 km) and accuracy (determination coefficient  $(\mathbb{R}^2)$  of 0.90, root mean square error (RMSE) of  $4.82~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ) (Fig. S3), compared with other publicly available  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  datasets for assessing  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  change and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable deaths in detail.

content.text: Limitations in both data acquisition and method selection should be acknowledged. First, the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs was investigated over a time span of 7 years, owing to the AQI data is available since 2013. The future study would be enriched with broader periods to observe a long-term performance. For method selection, the first limitation is that only CNN and LSTM were applied, ensemble-based approaches would be explored and incorporated along with the proposed model for further improvement in the prediction performance of the proposed framework. Further, the five socioeconomic factors were extracted and analyzed based on existing research and empirical knowledge. Future studies should continue to examine the deeper mechanisms of CCTPs using more quantitative and modelling methods.

args.query_intent: MEIC emissions inventory limitations

orchestrator_plan.reason: To directly focus on the known challenges and potential improvements associated with using MEIC emissions inventories for assessing air pollution trends, including rural and urban distinctions in China.
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
The retrieval task centered on exploring how to effectively assess the sources, mitigation measures, and environmental health impacts of air pollution in China. The specific area of focus was the MEIC (Multi-resolution Emission Inventory for China) emissions inventory, with the intent of investigating its limitations and strengths when analyzing air pollution trends across both rural and urban areas.

### Strategy and Execution
The retrieval strategy employed was **strategy_exact_search**, which aimed to directly target relevant data concerning the MEIC emissions inventory by searching for information tied to its potential limitations. The search parameters specified a limit of up to 20 results, with a sharp focus on uncovering insights related to known challenges in air pollution assessments, particularly using MEIC data across diverse geographic settings. Despite this precise approach, the effort yielded **no relevant hits**—there were zero results retrieved. 

### Retrieval Outcome
The lack of hits suggests several potential shortcomings in the execution:
1. **Query Scope**: The specificity of the query, while intended to ensure precision, may have restricted results due to overly narrow search criteria or mismatched terminology. It would be helpful to broaden or refine the query using synonyms or variations related to emissions inventory or environmental health impacts in China.
2. **Database Coverage**: The database leveraged for the search might not have indexed the type of content sought, particularly detailed analyses around MEIC inventory limitations and rural-urban air pollution patterns. 
3. **Complexity of Niche Topics**: Research and data concerning nuanced facets like emission inventory limitations often reside in highly specialized publications, which may not align well with a general retrieval algorithm unless the sources are explicitly curated.

### Evaluation of Retrieval Quality
- **Relevance**: The strategy demonstrated relevance by targeting MEIC emissions inventory limitations, which are critical for assessing air pollution. However, it failed to produce results, highlighting that the search approach may need expanded terminologies or adjusted focus (e.g., broader emissions tracking mechanisms or specific pollutants).
- **Accuracy**: No results were retrieved, so accuracy of information was not achieved. This indicates a potential gap in aligning the query intent with database capabilities or keyword matching.
- **Authority**: Without retrieved data, there is no opportunity to assess the credibility or authority of sources. This void stresses the importance of reconsidering search tools or databases that are rich in peer-reviewed environmental studies.
- **Completeness**: The search did not yield any insights, underscoring a lack of retrieval completeness. To achieve better outcomes, a more expansive search methodology or complementary retrieval strategies could be considered.

### Key Considerations and Implications
While the retrieval process was unsuccessful in surfacing actionable insights into the limitations of the MEIC emissions inventory, it highlights critical learning points for future efforts:
1. **Improved Search Query Design**: Revising the search query to incorporate broader language or alternative formulations (e.g., "China emissions data limitations," "air pollution modeling weaknesses") may connect to relevant sources.
2. **Database Selection**: Exploring databases that specialize in environmental science research or emissions analysis is crucial. Platforms like Web of Science, Scopus, or domain-specific government, NGO, or academic repositories could be beneficial.
3. **Complementary Strategies**: Iterative processes combining exact searches with broader exploratory searches could improve hit rates. Incorporating strategy variations, like fuzzy searches or citation networks, may find peripheral sources that are equally valuable.

In conclusion, this attempt to retrieve insights about assessments of air pollution in China via the MEIC emissions inventory encountered a dead end, but it serves as an instructive example of the challenges and constraints in searching for niche topics. The root objective remains critical, and improving retrieval tactics—whether through query expansion, database diversification, or multi-strategy searches—should animate subsequent efforts.

args.query_intent: long-term datasets or models tracking the impact of China's Air Clean Plan on public health outcomes post-2018

orchestrator_plan.reason: The follow-up should explore datasets and models specifically capable of measuring long-term trends in mitigation outcomes linked to the Air Clean Plan's implementation and public health impact, addressing gaps in prior knowledge.
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
To address the query on effectively assessing the sources, mitigation effectiveness, and environmental health impacts of air pollution in China—specifically in relation to long-term data and outcomes following the implementation of the country's Air Clean Plan—a semantic search strategy was employed. The search targeted 20 results and focused particularly on datasets, models, and findings that illustrate long-term trends in pollution reduction and the corresponding effects on public health post-2018. This approach was chosen to provide a more targeted view of air quality and its broader implications during and after the plan’s implementation.

### Key Findings and Context

#### China's Air Clean Plan: Phases and Outcomes
China’s Air Clean Plan has been a cornerstone of its air pollution mitigation strategy since 2013. The plan comprises two distinct phases:
1. **The Action Plan for Air Pollution Prevention and Control (APPCAP)** (2013–2017): Focused on reducing PM2.5 concentrations in highly polluted regions such as the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei (BTH) area, the Yangtze River Delta (YRD), and the Pearl River Delta (PRD).
2. **The Three-Year Action Plan for Winning the Blue Sky Defense (APWBSD)** (2018–2020): Aimed to expand upon the regional successes of APPCAP with broader, national-level air quality improvement targets.

Data from the query corroborates that these programs succeeded in meaningfully reducing air pollution levels, with an impressive 33.65% average reduction in PM2.5 concentrations nationwide between 2014 and 2020. Region-specific data from the BTH, YRD, and PRD regions revealed even sharper declines during 2013–2018, with PM2.5 levels falling by 40%, 34%, and 28%, respectively.

#### Data and Models on Pollution and Environmental Impacts
The retrieval results also highlighted relevant datasets. One significant dataset includes China’s 2012 emission inventory for industrial sources, which provides detailed annual emissions information for eight key pollutants across national, provincial, and city levels. Such records are instrumental in offering a baseline for assessing the effectiveness of measures like the Air Clean Plan.

While some records were deemed less useful due to their lack of substantive data or actionable insights (as noted with results rated with lower relevance scores during evaluation), others stood out for their logical presentation of findings and their applicability to the query at hand. For example, one highlighted result provided a detailed breakdown of pollution reduction, solidly linking the initiatives to measurable improvements in air quality.

#### Remaining Gaps and Considerations
Notably, while the reductions in PM2.5 concentrations were well-documented, there appears to be a relative scarcity of direct insights on the resulting public health outcomes post-2018. This indicates a gap in the available studies or in their capacity to associate improved air quality with specific health metrics over the long term. The search outcomes suggest that while environmental health impacts, such as reduced respiratory illnesses or cardiovascular diseases, are relevant to the overarching question, more focused or robust studies might be required to directly correlate the Air Clean Plan with such public health metrics.

Additionally, the results point to the potential need for more advanced models to assess the full ecosystem impacts, including those that link air pollution reduction to broader health outcomes across geographic scales and timespans.

### Retrieval Analysis and Evaluation
The overall retrieval yielded 20 results, with varying levels of applicability:
- **Relevance**: Several results directly addressed key metrics like PM2.5 reduction (e.g., baseline datasets, regional reduction insights). However, a subset of results proved repetitive or lacked actionable insights, merely outlining research topics without providing new data or conclusions.
- **Accuracy/Authority**: High-scoring sources tended to use robust methodologies and originated from credible institutional research, lending confidence to their findings on emissions, PM2.5 reductions, and by extension, their implications for public health.
- **Completeness**: While the search gathered essential insights on the effectiveness of government policies in mitigating air pollution and some baseline data on industrial emissions, gaps in health-focused studies and long-term impacts were evident. 

### Summary and Recommendations
The retrieval process effectively highlights that China's Air Clean Plan has had a significant impact on air quality, as evidenced by substantive reductions in PM2.5 levels in both key regions and at the national level. Credible datasets, such as emission inventories from 2012, provide a foundation for assessing the outcomes of these environmental policies. However, despite these successes, the search also uncovered a gap in directly linking air quality improvements to long-term public health outcomes, particularly after 2018 when the second phase of the program came into effect.

To comprehensively assess the sources and long-term health impacts of air pollution mitigation efforts in China, a more tailored focus on public health datasets, epidemiological studies, and advanced multi-factor models that account for regional differences and lagged health impacts is recommended. Conducting further studies and extending searches to new databases or gray literature may help to capture these missing dimensions and contribute to a more comprehensive understanding of China's air quality management efforts.
orchestrator_plan.hyde_hypothetical_paragraph_full_text: Recent studies leveraging long-term datasets and advanced atmospheric modeling have provided critical insights into the public health impacts of China's Air Clean Plan (ACP) post-2018. For instance, integrated exposure-response models combined with satellite-derived PM2.5 data have demonstrated significant reductions in premature mortality attributable to ischemic heart disease, stroke, and chronic respiratory diseases in urban regions. Additionally, longitudinal epidemiological analyses using national health registries have revealed a marked decline in hospital admissions for respiratory ailments, correlating with sustained decreases in ambient PM2.5 concentrations. These findings underscore the effectiveness of the ACP in mitigating air pollution-related health burdens, while highlighting the need for continued monitoring to assess long-term trends and regional disparities.

content.text: After seven-year implementing of Air Clean Plan, the mean  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations clearly decreased  $(13.41\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ $33.65\%)$  in China. The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in the first phase (2014-2017) were 35.97  $\pm 15.11~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ , which decreased to  $28.92\pm 12.76~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$  in the latter phase (2018-2020) of the China ACP. In both phases, high concentrations were observed mainly in northwest China, northern China, and a small proportion of central China (i.e., Henan and Hubei provinces) (Fig. 1). The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations decreased in over  $68.09\%$  of the area in mainland China during the period of APPCAP (Fig. 1b). A clear reduction was detected in BTH (decreased by  $17.33~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $25.81\%$ ), followed by YRD (decreased by  $12.79~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $24.07\%$ ) and parts of central China (decreased by  $14.66~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $22.93\%$ ) during the APPCAP

content.text: China's past economic growth has substantially relied on fossil fuels, causing serious air pollution issues. Decoupling economic growth and pollution has become the focus in developing ecological civilization in China. We have analyzed the three-decade progress of air pollution controls in China, highlighting a strategic transformation from emission control toward air quality management. Emission control of sulfur dioxide  $(\mathrm{SO}_2)$  resolved the deteriorating acid rain issue in China in 2007. Since 2013, control actions on multiple precursors and sectors have targeted the reduction of the concentration of fine particulate matter  $(\mathrm{PM}_{2.5})$ , marking a transition to an air-quality-oriented strategy. Increasing ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  pollution further requires  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  integrated control strategies with an emphasis on their complex photochemical interactions. Fundamental improvement of air quality in China, as a key indicator for the success of ecological civilization construction, demands the deep de-carbonization of China's energy system as well as more synergistic pathways to address air pollution and global climate change simultaneously. © 2020 THE AUTHORS. Published by Elsevier LTD on behalf of Chinese Academy of Engineering and Higher Education Press Limited Company. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license

content.text: We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\sim 27.05\%$  of global ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\%$  and  $26\%$  decrease in the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors.

content.text: China implemented a stringent Air Clean Plan (ACP) since 2013 to address environmental and health risks caused by ambient fine particulate matter  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . However, the policy effectiveness of ACP and co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures to environment and health are still largely unknown. Using satellite-based  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products produced in our previous study, concentration-response functions, and the logarithmic mean Divisia index (LMDI) method, we analyzed the spatiotemporal dynamics of premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and quantitatively estimated the policy benefits of ACP and carbon mitigation measures. We found the annual  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in China decreased by  $33.65\%$ $(13.41\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  from 2014 to 2020, accompanied by a decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths of 0.23 million  $(95\%$  confidence interval (CI): 0.22-0.27), indicating the huge benefits of China ACP for human health and environment. However, there were still 1.12 million  $(95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by the exposure of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in mainland China in 2020. Among all ACP measures, clean production (contributed  $55.98\%$  and  $51.14\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) and energy consumption control (contributed  $32.58\%$  and  $29.54\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) made the largest contribution during the past seven years. Nevertheless, the environmental and health benefits of ACP are not fully synergistic in different regions, and the effectiveness of ACP measures reduced from 2018 to 2020. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  has become one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction since 2018, confirming the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Our study suggests, with the saturation of clean production and source control, more targeted region-specific strategies and synergistic air pollution-carbon mitigation measures are critical to achieving the WHO's Air Quality Guideline target and the UN's Sustainable Development Goal Target in China.

content.text: Our study estimated the changes in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in China at a fine-resolution (1 km) during 2014-2020, identifying the measure with greatest environmental and health co-benefits in ACP, and demonstrating the potential of carbon mitigation measures for air quality improvement and public health enhancement. Results showed that (1) the average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in China decreased by  $33.65\%$  ( $13.41~\mu \mathrm{g}~\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) from 2014 to 2020, accompanied by a decrease in total attributed deaths of 0.23 million (95 % CI: 0.22-0.27), indicating the great benefits of China ACP for human health and environment. However, the premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China still reached 1.12 million (95 % CI: 0.79-1.56) in 2020. (2) Among China ACP measures, clean production and energy consumption control made the largest contribution to air quality improvements and health burden mitigation during the past seven years. However, the effectiveness of the two ACP measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. Meanwhile, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic spatially. (3) The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  has become one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase, confirming the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures.

content.text: The Global Burden of Disease Study (GBD) 2015 identified ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure as a significant risk factor in the disease burden in China and as responsible for 1.1 million deaths in that year [16]. Based on the GBD project, the World Bank Group estimated that the premature mortality costs due to air pollution (including indoor exposure) were close to  $10\%$  of the GDP equivalent in 2013—significantly higher than those of developed countries in Europe and North America [17]. Undoubtedly, given the scale of the impacts of air pollution on public health, improvement of air quality has become a central domain in developing ecological civilization in China.

content.text: Air pollution is one of the greatest environmental problems the world faces today, leading to significant undesirable effects. Being the greatest environmental risk to health, air pollution threatens human health by increasing the morbidity and mortality of respiratory disease, heart disease, lung cancer, diabetes, etc. [1-3], contributing to estimated 7 million premature deaths each year [4]. Although typical air pollutants in China have decreased significantly in recent years, air pollution in China remains inferior to that experienced on average globally. According to the latest 2018 reports released by the IQAir [5] and Greenpeace [6], China ranks third in  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions and twelfth in annual average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations globally.
