Schematic methodology of the MIX emission inventory development.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\\figure_2.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Methodology & Design", "sub_roles": ["Illustrate Algorithm Process"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Diagrams & Schematics", "sub_types": ["Flowchart"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX inventory", "MEIC", "PKU-NH3", "ANL-India", "REAS2", "CAPSS", "SMOKE-Asia", "speciation scheme"], "concise_summary": "The figure illustrates the process of developing the MIX Asian emission inventory using various regional datasets and regridding methods.", "inferred_insight": "The schematic highlights the integration of multiple regional emission data sources into the MIX inventory, showcasing a versatile approach in emission data compilation.", "references": ["Figure 2 illustrates the mosaic process for the MIX inventory development. Each dataset was reprocessed to  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution with monthly variations when necessary. We used monthly gridded emissions from each component inventory where available and assumed no monthly variation in emissions when the component inventory only provided annual emissions. The monthly profiles and spatial proxies used in each component emission inventories are summarized in Tables S3 and S4.", "# MIX: a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission inventory under the international collaboration framework of the MICS-Asia and HTAP", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Recent studies (e.g., Zhang et al., 2009; Kurokawa et al., 2013) tend to use the mosaic approach to supplement the Asian emission inventory developments. To support the NASA's INTEX-B (the Intercontinental Chemical Transport Experiment Phase B) mission (van Donkelaar et al., 2008; Adhikary et al., 2010), Zhang et al. (2009) developed a new emission inventory for Asia for the year 2006 as an update and improvement of the TRACE-P inventory (Streets et al., 2003). Compared to the TRACE-P inventory, the INTEX-B inventory improved emission estimates for China by introducing a technology-based methodology and incorporated several local inventories including BC and OC emissions for India from Reddy et al. (2002a, b), a Japan emission inventory from Kannari et al. (2007), and official emission inventories for the Republic of Korea and Taiwan. In the updated version 2.1 of the REAS inventory (Kurokawa et al., 2013), a few regional inventories developed with local knowledge are also incorporated to improve the accuracy (see Sect. 2.2.1 for details).", "In order to support the MICS-Asia III and other global and regional modeling activities with the best available anthropogenic emission dataset over Asia, we develop a new Asian anthropogenic emission inventory, named MIX, by harmonizing different local emission inventories with the mosaic approach. The mosaic inventory developed in this work will provide (1) a more complete and state-of-the-art understanding of anthropogenic emissions over Asia with best estimates from local inventories; (2) a reference dataset with moderate accuracy and resolution that can support both scientific research and mitigation policy-making; and (3) broader application of the best available local inventories in modeling studies by processing them to model-ready format and including them in a publicly available emission dataset.", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1. Domain and component of the MIX emission inventory.", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "# 2 Compilation of the MIX emission inventory", "We then selected different emission datasets for various species for each country by the following hierarchy. REAS2 was used as the default where local emission data are absent. Emission inventories compiled by the official agencies or developed with more local information are selected to override REAS2, which include MEIC for mainland China, ANL-India for India, and CAPSS for the Republic of Korea. Detailed information and advantages of these inventories are presented in Sect. 2.2. As only a few species  $(\\mathrm{SO}_2,$  BC, OC, and power plant  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  were available from ANL-India, REAS2 was used to supplement the missing species. A mosaic process was then used to combine ANL-India and REAS2 into a single dataset for Indian emissions. It is worth noting that the REAS2 has incorporated local inventories for Japan and Taiwan, which are subsequently adopted in MIX for these two regions. PKU-NH<sub>3</sub> was further used to replace MEIC emissions for NH<sub>3</sub> over China, given that PKU-NH<sub>3</sub> was developed with a process-based model that represented the spatiotemporal variations in NH<sub>3</sub> emissions. Table 2 lists the information of each inventory used in MIX.", "Table 1. Summary of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory.", "Figure 3. NMVOC speciation scheme used in the MIX inventory development. The mapping table is derived from Carter (2013).", "# 2.2 Components of the MIX emission inventory", "The REAS2 inventory is provided with monthly gridded emission data for both air pollutants and  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  by sectors at  $0.25\\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution. We aggregated the 11 REAS2 sectors to 5 sectors provided in the MIX inventory. Emissions from open biomass burning, aviation, and international shipping were excluded from the REAS2 before incorporating into MIX. Monthly variations are developed for power plants, industry, residential sources, and cold-start emissions from vehicles by various monthly profiles (Kurokawa et al., 2013). In REAS2, power plants with annual  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions larger than  $1\\mathrm{Tg}$  were provided as point sources with coordinates of locations, while emissions for other sectors were processed as areal sources and gridded at  $0.25\\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution using maps of rural, urban, and total populations and road networks (see Table S4).", "We use anthropogenic emission data generated from the MEIC (Multi-resolution Emission Inventory for China) model to override emissions in mainland China. MEIC is a bottom-up emission inventory framework developed and maintained by Tsinghua University, which uses a technology-based methodology to calculate air pollutant and  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions for more than 700 anthropogenic emitting sources for China from 1990 to the present. With the detailed source classification, the MEIC model can represent emission characteristics from different sectors, fuels, products, combustion/process technologies, and emission control technologies. The MEIC model improved the bottom-up emission inventories developed by the same group (Streets et al., 2006; Zhang et al., 2007a, b, 2009; Lei et al., 2011) and integrated them into a uniform framework. The major improvements include a unit-based power plant emission database (Wang et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2015), a high-resolution vehicle emission modeling approach (Zheng et al., 2014), an explicit NMVOC speciation assignment methodology (Li et al., 2014), and a unified, online framework for emission calculation, data processing, and data downloading (available at http://www.meicmodel.org).", "We used a high-resolution  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  emission inventory in China compiled by Peking University (PKU-NH $_3$ ; Huang et al., 2012) to replace China's  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions in MEIC. MEIC used annual and regional average  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  emission factors to calculate emissions from each source category, while PKU-NH $_3$  used a process-based model to estimate  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions which parameterized the spatial and temporal variations of emission factors with consideration of ambient temperature, soil property, and other factors. For  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions from fertilizer applications, fertilizer type, soil property, fertilizer application method, application rate, and ambient temperature were used to develop monthly and gridded emission factors. For livestock wastes, emissions were estimated based on a mass-flow methodology by tracing the migration and volatilization of nitrogen from each stage of livestock manure management.", "A high-resolution Indian emission inventory developed by ANL (referred to as ANL-India hereafter; Lu et al., 2011) was used in the MIX inventory. ANL-India used a technology-based methodology to estimate  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC emissions in India for the period of 1996-2010. Major anthropogenic sources including both fossil-fuel and biofuel combustion are covered in ANL-India. Time-dependent trends in emission factors were developed by taking account of the impact of technology changes on emissions (Habib et al., 2004; Venkataraman et al., 2005). Lu and Streets (2012) further updated power plant emissions in India by calculating emissions at the generating unit level ( $\\sim$ 800 units in total) based on information from the reports of the Central Electricity Authority (CEA), including geographical location, capacity, fuel type, electricity generation, time the plant was commissioned/decommissioned, etc. The exact location of each power plant was obtained from the Global Energy Observatory (http://globalenergyobservatory.org) and crosschecked through Google Earth. The updated unit-based power plant emissions in ANL-India are available for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , BC, and OC.", "For the Republic of Korea, we used the CAPSS emission inventory developed by the National Institute of Environmental Research of Korea (Lee et al., 2011). CAPSS estimated emissions with four levels of source classifications. We mapped emissions from 12 first-level aggregated source categories (SCC1) to five sectors in MIX. The CAPSS inventory included emissions for  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and five regulated air pollutants,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC, and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ . We derived sector-specific emission ratios between  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and the other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011) and applied those", "ratios to estimate  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions. In the MIX inventory, we used the 2008 and 2009 CAPSS inventories to represent 2008 and 2010 emissions of the Republic of Korea, because 2009 is the most recent year of CAPSS inventory at the time the MIX inventory was developed. In the CAPSS inventory, point sources, area sources, and mobile sources were processed using different spatial allocation approaches (Lee et al., 2011). We used the  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  emission product from CAPSS as input for the MIX inventory. Only annual total emissions were presented in the CAPSS inventory. In the MIX inventory, we assume no monthly variation in emissions in the Republic of Korea.", "We directly used monthly emissions from each regional emission inventory when compiling the MIX inventory. We assume no monthly variation in emissions when monthly profiles are absent from the regional emission inventories. Table S3 presents the monthly profiles used in each component emission inventory for MIX. In summary, monthly profiles for power plant emissions usually developed based on monthly statistics of power generation. Monthly profiles of industrial emissions are derived from monthly output of industrial products or industrial GDP. Residential monthly profiles are estimated from stove operation time based on ambient temperatures by regions (Streets et al., 2003).", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2 / \\mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\\mathrm{CO} / \\mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2 / \\mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{CO} / \\mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2 / \\mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "A comprehensive intercomparison among different emission inventories over Asia was conducted by Kurokawa et al. (2013). In this work, we compare the MIX inventory", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\\%$  for most species, except for  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\\%$  higher in REAS),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "ences by region and by sector. Regionally, the differences can be largely attributed to disagreements in emission estimates for China and India, as presented in Table 7. Discrepancies are relatively large at the sector level compared to total emissions. EDGAR's estimates for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions from power plants are  $60\\%$  higher than estimates in MIX. For China,  $70\\%$  of power generation capacities were equipped with FGD and the average  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  removal efficiency was  $78\\%$  (Liu et al., 2015). The high estimates in EDGAR v4.2 are most likely due to underestimation of FGD penetration or  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  removal efficiencies of FGD (Kurokawa et al., 2013). Remarkable differences for the residential and transportation sectors are found for  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, and NMVOC estimates in the two inventories. For instance, EDGAR v4.2 estimates lower  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions of transportation sector by 27 and by  $48\\%$  for the residential sector compared to MIX. Similarly, residential CO emissions in EDGAR v4.2 are  $33\\%$  lower than in MIX, leading to  $33\\%$  lower estimates of CO emissions in EDGAR v4.2 compared to MIX. Underestimates of CO emissions in EDGAR v4.2 inventory have been confirmed by top-down constraints (Pétron et al., 2004; Fortems-Cheiney et al., 2011). As the statistical differences of energy use are usually within  $30\\%$  at sector level (Guan et al., 2012), the discrepancy by sector could only be attributed to differences in the raw emission factors and abatement measures. Al-", "+13.6% compared to MIX) but lower emissions for industry  $(-293\\mathrm{Tg}, -6.8\\%)$  emissions. EDGAR v4.2 estimates lower  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions for China  $(-308\\mathrm{Tg}, -3.4\\%)$  and higher emissions for other regions: +102Tg (+5.6%) for Other East Asia, +83.9Tg (+5.6%) for Southeast Asia, +204Tg (+9.7%) for India, +29.5Tg (+7.5%) for Other South Asia, and +25.3Tg (+6.4%) for Central Asia. At sector level, EDGAR v4.2 estimates are 29% (+833 Tg) higher for power, 22% (-944Tg) lower for industry, and 31% (-192 Tg) lower for transportation over China compared to MIX. For Other East Asia, differences between EDGAR v4.2 and MIX are mainly contributed by power sector, with 20% higher emissions (+130 Tg) in EDGAR v4.2. Residential sector is the main contributor to the differences between EDGAR v4.2 and MIX over India, with 28% (+177 Tg) higher emissions estimated in EDGAR v4.2. The relatively large discrepancy at sector level can be attributed to differences in energy statistics and emission factors (Guan et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2015) as well as differences in sector definitions. In particularly, EDGAR v4.2 used fuel consumption data from IEA statistics while MIX and REAS2 used provincial level data from Chinese Energy Statistics, which can differ by 20% at sector level (Hong et al., 2016). Emissions from heating plants are aggregated to the industrial sector in the MIX inventory, while in EDGAR v4.2 heating plants", "are aggregated to the energy sector and then compared to the power sector in the MIX inventory. In the future, harmonizing the sector (subsector) definition among global and regional inventories would help to reduce the discrepancy of emission estimates at sector level.", "The agriculture sector is a dominant source of  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions, mainly contributed by fertilizer applications and manure managements. MIX incorporated the PKU-NH $_3$  inventory for China, which estimated agricultural  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions using a process-based model to represent the dynamic impact of fertilizer use patterns, meteorological factors, and soil properties (Huang et al., 2012). The new inventory improved on previous studies which used uniform emission factors across time and region. Table 8 compares agricultural  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions in China estimated in different emission inventories. Compared to other work, PKU-NH $_3$  yields lower estimates for fertilizer application but higher estimates for manure management. The differences are mainly because PKU-NH $_3$  used local correction factors for fertilizer volatilization and manure loss rate (Huang et al., 2012). Top-down inversion of  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions by adjoint model and deposition fluxes agrees well with Huang et al. (2012), confirming the validity of the process-based model (Paulot et al., 2014).", "This section further discusses the differences between MIX and REAS2 over China. EDGAR is not compared here because references to the detailed underlying data used in EDGAR are not available. Figure S1 in the Supplement compares MIX and REAS2 estimates for China for 2008 by species and by sector. The two inventories generally agree well given that both MEIC and REAS2 incorporate the most recent advances in emission inventory studies in China. The major differences between the two inventories are discussed below with explanation for possible reasons.", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "For a mosaic emission inventory, inconsistencies could occur at country boarders when emissions of the two adjacent countries were obtained from different datasets (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015). In the MIX inventory, the inconsistencies are expected at the country boarder of China and India. However, low populations and emissions are observed along the border of China, reducing the impact of cross-border grids on the accuracy of emissions. Also, deriving country totals from the gridded emissions is not appropriate for small countries due to the impact from cross-border grids, especially for those grids with large point source emissions (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015).", "The current MIX inventory also has several limitations. Firstly, it provides emissions with aggregated sectoral information, which may be sufficient for the base case model but insufficient for targeted policy cases. Secondly, the MIX inventory is provided with moderate spatial resolution (i.e.,  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$ ), which could support global and regional models but is still too coarse for urban models. Finally, yet importantly, gridded emissions are only available for 2008 and 2010 to support base years modeling activities in MICS-Asia and HTAP. For other years, modelers could use available global and regional inventories with more complete year coverage (e.g., EDGAR v4.2, REAS2) or extrapolate the gridded MIX inventory to the neighboring years. Developing a complete time series of gridded emission dataset with the", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "The MIX inventory provides a consistent emission input for Asian regions for global and regional modeling activities. We expect that the MIX inventory can provide a good foundation for air quality modeling and can help to improve the model performance. However, the MIX inventory still has some limitations. It is very difficult to conduct quantitative uncertainty analysis for a mosaic inventory, which limits understanding of the reliability of the MIX inventory. Validation of the MIX inventory could be provided by comparing model predictions with in situ and satellite observations. source_args.query_intent: What are the uncertainties or limitations in the MIX emission inventory for rural emissions, transboundary pollution, or underrepresented sectors, and how can this inventory be improved for assessing air quality in China and neighboring countries?

evaluation.extracted_insight: The schematic highlights the integration of localized emission datasets into the MIX inventory, fostering harmonized regional data sets for modeling applications.
evaluation.reason: Valuable methodology visualization for emission data integration with clear application relevance.

content.text: Figure 1 illustrates the geographic distribution of components within the MIX emission inventory across Asian countries and regions, detailing specific datasets for each area. The figure provides a visual representation of how different regional inventories are integrated within the MIX framework, demonstrating the collaborative approach across countries for comprehensive emission data.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_154", "figure_title": "Figure 1. Domain and component of the MIX emission inventory.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX emission inventory", "REAS2", "MEIC", "JEI-DB", "OPRF", "CAPSS", "PKU-NH3", "ANL-India"], "concise_summary": "Figure 1 illustrates the geographic distribution of components within the MIX emission inventory across Asian countries and regions, detailing specific datasets for each area.", "inferred_insight": "The figure provides a visual representation of how different regional inventories are integrated within the MIX framework, demonstrating the collaborative approach across countries for comprehensive emission data.", "references": ["Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "# MIX: a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission inventory under the international collaboration framework of the MICS-Asia and HTAP", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "In order to support the MICS-Asia III and other global and regional modeling activities with the best available anthropogenic emission dataset over Asia, we develop a new Asian anthropogenic emission inventory, named MIX, by harmonizing different local emission inventories with the mosaic approach. The mosaic inventory developed in this work will provide (1) a more complete and state-of-the-art understanding of anthropogenic emissions over Asia with best estimates from local inventories; (2) a reference dataset with moderate accuracy and resolution that can support both scientific research and mitigation policy-making; and (3) broader application of the best available local inventories in modeling studies by processing them to model-ready format and including them in a publicly available emission dataset.", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "# 2 Compilation of the MIX emission inventory", "We then selected different emission datasets for various species for each country by the following hierarchy. REAS2 was used as the default where local emission data are absent. Emission inventories compiled by the official agencies or developed with more local information are selected to override REAS2, which include MEIC for mainland China, ANL-India for India, and CAPSS for the Republic of Korea. Detailed information and advantages of these inventories are presented in Sect. 2.2. As only a few species  $(\\mathrm{SO}_2,$  BC, OC, and power plant  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  were available from ANL-India, REAS2 was used to supplement the missing species. A mosaic process was then used to combine ANL-India and REAS2 into a single dataset for Indian emissions. It is worth noting that the REAS2 has incorporated local inventories for Japan and Taiwan, which are subsequently adopted in MIX for these two regions. PKU-NH<sub>3</sub> was further used to replace MEIC emissions for NH<sub>3</sub> over China, given that PKU-NH<sub>3</sub> was developed with a process-based model that represented the spatiotemporal variations in NH<sub>3</sub> emissions. Table 2 lists the information of each inventory used in MIX.", "Figure 2 illustrates the mosaic process for the MIX inventory development. Each dataset was reprocessed to  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution with monthly variations when necessary. We used monthly gridded emissions from each component inventory where available and assumed no monthly variation in emissions when the component inventory only provided annual emissions. The monthly profiles and spatial proxies used in each component emission inventories are summarized in Tables S3 and S4.", "Table 1. Summary of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory.", "Figure 2. Schematic methodology of the MIX emission inventory development.", "# 2.2 Components of the MIX emission inventory", "The REAS2 inventory is provided with monthly gridded emission data for both air pollutants and  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  by sectors at  $0.25\\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution. We aggregated the 11 REAS2 sectors to 5 sectors provided in the MIX inventory. Emissions from open biomass burning, aviation, and international shipping were excluded from the REAS2 before incorporating into MIX. Monthly variations are developed for power plants, industry, residential sources, and cold-start emissions from vehicles by various monthly profiles (Kurokawa et al., 2013). In REAS2, power plants with annual  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions larger than  $1\\mathrm{Tg}$  were provided as point sources with coordinates of locations, while emissions for other sectors were processed as areal sources and gridded at  $0.25\\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution using maps of rural, urban, and total populations and road networks (see Table S4).", "A high-resolution Indian emission inventory developed by ANL (referred to as ANL-India hereafter; Lu et al., 2011) was used in the MIX inventory. ANL-India used a technology-based methodology to estimate  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC emissions in India for the period of 1996-2010. Major anthropogenic sources including both fossil-fuel and biofuel combustion are covered in ANL-India. Time-dependent trends in emission factors were developed by taking account of the impact of technology changes on emissions (Habib et al., 2004; Venkataraman et al., 2005). Lu and Streets (2012) further updated power plant emissions in India by calculating emissions at the generating unit level ( $\\sim$ 800 units in total) based on information from the reports of the Central Electricity Authority (CEA), including geographical location, capacity, fuel type, electricity generation, time the plant was commissioned/decommissioned, etc. The exact location of each power plant was obtained from the Global Energy Observatory (http://globalenergyobservatory.org) and crosschecked through Google Earth. The updated unit-based power plant emissions in ANL-India are available for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , BC, and OC.", "For the Republic of Korea, we used the CAPSS emission inventory developed by the National Institute of Environmental Research of Korea (Lee et al., 2011). CAPSS estimated emissions with four levels of source classifications. We mapped emissions from 12 first-level aggregated source categories (SCC1) to five sectors in MIX. The CAPSS inventory included emissions for  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and five regulated air pollutants,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC, and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ . We derived sector-specific emission ratios between  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and the other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011) and applied those", "ratios to estimate  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions. In the MIX inventory, we used the 2008 and 2009 CAPSS inventories to represent 2008 and 2010 emissions of the Republic of Korea, because 2009 is the most recent year of CAPSS inventory at the time the MIX inventory was developed. In the CAPSS inventory, point sources, area sources, and mobile sources were processed using different spatial allocation approaches (Lee et al., 2011). We used the  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  emission product from CAPSS as input for the MIX inventory. Only annual total emissions were presented in the CAPSS inventory. In the MIX inventory, we assume no monthly variation in emissions in the Republic of Korea.", "We directly used monthly emissions from each regional emission inventory when compiling the MIX inventory. We assume no monthly variation in emissions when monthly profiles are absent from the regional emission inventories. Table S3 presents the monthly profiles used in each component emission inventory for MIX. In summary, monthly profiles for power plant emissions usually developed based on monthly statistics of power generation. Monthly profiles of industrial emissions are derived from monthly output of industrial products or industrial GDP. Residential monthly profiles are estimated from stove operation time based on ambient temperatures by regions (Streets et al., 2003).", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. content.text: Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$

content.text: Based on our review of the spatial proxies used in bottom-up emission inventories for China, it can be concluded that such spatial proxies are empirical and may have introduced considerable uncertainties into the spatial distributions of emissions. Studies on fossil fuel  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  inventories have made tremendous efforts to improve the spatial distribution of emissions based on satellite-derived nighttime light (Oda et al., 2011), fuel sales and traffic data (McDonald et al., 2014), multivariate regressions (Wang et al., 2013; Gately et al., 2015), or combined fossil fuel data assimilation systems (Rayner et al., 2010; Asefi-Najafabady et al., 2014), shedding new light on ways to improve the spatial distribution of air pollutant inventories.

content.text: Abstract. Spatial proxies used in bottom-up emission inventories to derive the spatial distributions of emissions are usually empirical and involve additional levels of uncertainty. Although uncertainties in current emission inventories have been discussed extensively, uncertainties resulting from improper spatial proxies have rarely been evaluated. In this work, we investigate the impact of spatial proxies on the representation of gridded emissions by comparing six gridded  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission datasets over China developed from the same magnitude of emissions and different spatial proxies. GEOS-Chem-modeled tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns simulated from different gridded emission inventories are compared with satellite-based columns. The results show that differences between modeled and satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns are sensitive to the spatial proxies used in the gridded emission inventories. The total population density is less suitable for allocating  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions than nighttime light data because population density tends to allocate more emissions to rural areas. Determining the exact locations of large emission sources could significantly strengthen the correlation between modeled and observed  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns. Using vehicle population and an updated road network for the on-road transport sector could substantially en

content.text: different inventories and providing emission data with uniform format. Available emission inventories always differ in geographic region, time period, source classification, species, and spatial and temporal resolution, introducing complexities in intercomparisons of emissions and model results with different emission inputs. By involving the state-of-the-art local emission inventories developed with local knowledge and harmonizing them to uniform format, this approach can provide a reference on magnitude and spatial distribution of emissions for different regions, while there is always tradeoff in spatial-temporal coverage and resolution due to inconsistencies among involved inventories.

content.text: Other factors, such as the resolution, may also introduce uncertainty. The spatial proxies in S6 are quite good at the resolution of  $0.667^{\circ}$  long  $\times 0.5^{\circ}$  lat used in this work, which roughly corresponds to the county level in eastern China. However, they may not be suitable at other resolutions. Further work based on models with finer grids should be performed to explore appropriate spatial proxies at finer resolutions. Natural emissions from lightning and soil sources are not discussed in this work, although they are suggested to be underestimated by approximately  $16\%$  for China for 2006; they account for less than 3 and  $6\%$  of anthropogenic emissions, respectively, and even less in highly polluted regions (Lin, 2012).

content.text: information on emission factors was identified to be crucial for the uncertainties of emissions for those source categories. The results indicated the importance of better understanding on emission characteristics of individual plants when emission inventory was developed using a "plant-based" method. Such experience could be extended to other regions/cities with similar economic and industrial structures. Due to lack of sufficient field measurements, however, the plant-specific emission factors for all species were still unavailable at local spatial scale for other industrial sources, and technology-based emission factors had to be assigned for all the plants of the same manufacturing technology types, without independent uncertainty assumptions. Moreover, the uncertainty analysis for sectors other than industry at provincial and city scales could hardly be improved, as data from current available statistics or investigations could not better differentiate the emission characteristics by source type or provide detailed information on errors of activity data and emission factors. Those limitations motivated more detailed on-site investigations and field measurements on typical emission sources for further improving the emission estimates at local scales.

args.query_intent: PM2.5-attributable lung cancer trends in elderly post-2018

orchestrator_plan.reason: To refine the focus on synergistic effects between PM2.5 reductions and demographic health impacts post-Air Clean Plan, particularly regarding lung cancer outcomes in aging populations after 2018.
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
### Retrieval Summary

The root goal of the analysis was to understand how to properly assess the sources, mitigation effectiveness, and environmental health impacts of air pollution in China. Specifically, the focus narrowed to examining trends related to PM2.5-attributed lung cancer in elderly populations after 2018. This time frame is significant as it follows the implementation of China's Air Clean Plan, a comprehensive policy aimed at reducing air pollution and improving public health outcomes. Understanding how PM2.5 reductions might have affected lung cancer rates in elderly populations can shed light on the interplay between environmental policy and demographic health impacts.

#### Strategy Description
The retrieval process employed an **exact search strategy**, aiming to precisely align retrieval results with the refined question's intent. The query explicitly targeted “PM2.5-attributable lung cancer trends in elderly populations post-2018,” requesting up to 20 search results. This approach sought to isolate high-quality and directly relevant data to address the intersection of fine particulate matter (PM2.5) pollution trends, health outcomes, and environmental policymaking in China since the noted temporal marker.

#### Retrieval Results
Despite a focused search strategy, the process yielded a **hit count of zero**. This indicates that no documents were retrieved specifically matching the query’s intent and criteria. As a result, the retrieval process failed to return meaningful observations or evidence that could address the question.

#### Analysis of Retrieval Quality
Several dimensions of retrieval quality are worth evaluating here:

**1. Relevance:**  
The search strategy itself was suitably formulated to target the query intent, demonstrating high relevance in its design. However, the absence of results suggests either a lack of indexed content addressing this specific topic or a potential misalignment in the phrasing or structuring of the search query relative to available resources.

**2. Accuracy:**  
Because no results were retrieved, accuracy cannot be assessed in terms of the content’s alignment with the query intent. However, the zero-hit outcome may highlight gaps in data availability or indexing practices on PM2.5-related health trends in post-2018 China.

**3. Authority:**  
With no search outcomes to evaluate, the credibility or authority of sources remains indeterminate. The expectation was to retrieve authoritative epidemiological or environmental studies, yet no such content emerged. This points to limitations either in the scope covered by indexed datasets or the specificity of the query itself.

**4. Completeness:**  
Completeness is the most significant limitation here. The total absence of retrieved results leaves the question entirely unanswered. This suggests potential gaps in data curation or a need for broader search strategies to ensure adequate coverage of the topic.

#### Key Insights
The retrieval attempt highlights significant barriers to locating and synthesizing information on PM2.5-associated health outcomes for elderly populations after 2018 in China. No insights could be derived from the trace itself since no evidence was retrieved.

#### Recommendations for Improvement:
Based on the challenges observed in this retrieval process, several strategies could be adopted to better address the root goal:

**1. Broaden the Query Scope:**  
The exact search approach, while precise, may have been overly restrictive. Reformulating the search query to allow for broader interpretation could surface more general data on PM2.5 health trends and demographic analysis in China post-2018, even if not explicitly tied to lung cancer rates in elderly populations.

**2. Pivot Search Intent:**  
If direct epidemiological studies on PM2.5-attributed lung cancer trends are not available, alternative angles might be considered. For example, searching for overall PM2.5 reduction metrics, broader health impacts (e.g., respiratory or cardiovascular outcomes), or environmental policy evaluations could provide context.

**3. Utilize Specialized Databases:**  
General-purpose search engines may not index the specific public health studies or datasets required. Deploying specialized databases like PubMed for medical studies or environmental research repositories may yield better results.

**4. Integrate Secondary Sources:**  
When direct evidence is unavailable, secondary analyses—such as government reports, meta-analyses, or regional studies—could provide proxy insights into PM2.5 reductions, policy effectiveness, and health impacts.

**5. Expand Temporal Scope:**  
Although the post-2018 timeframe is relevant, extending the scope to consider pre-2018 trends (pre-Air Clean Plan) may offer context for comparative analysis and thus enrich the assessment.

### Conclusion
The retrieval process attempted to tackle a highly focused and scientifically significant question but was ultimately unsuccessful due to the absence of relevant sources. The root goal—linking PM2.5 pollution trends to specific policy impacts and demographic health outcomes—remains unmet in this experiment. Moving forward, adopting broader search parameters and leveraging specialized databases could improve retrieval prospects and enable a more complete analysis.

sections.response: Emission inventories are vital tools for understanding and addressing air pollution, yet they are not without significant limitations. Bottom-up emission inventories often rely on empirical spatial proxies, which can introduce considerable uncertainties in the spatial distribution of emissions, particularly for specific sources like biomass burning or industrial outputs [chunk_002820; chunk_002888; chunk_003896]. For example, the use of traditional methods to estimate emissions has been shown to significantly vary depending on regional practices and available data, contributing to inconsistencies [chunk_001916; chunk_002985]. Efforts to refine these inventories through higher-resolution data, such as those produced using the MEIC model in China or optimized with satellite constraints, have demonstrated improvements in spatial accuracy and alignment with observed air quality data, though challenges in quantifying uncertainties remain [chunk_003013; chunk_004676; chunk_003783]. The need for harmonization across regional and sectoral datasets is critical, as the heterogeneity in inventory formats, geographic scales, and temporal resolutions complicates global and regional modeling comparisons [chunk_002649; chunk_002987]. Overall, while advancements are being made, continual refinement of methodologies and integration of diverse data sources are essential to mitigate the uncertainties inherent in emission inventory development.

sections.step_name: Integration of Satellite and Ground-Based Methods
sections.response: The integration of satellite and ground-based methods has become crucial in improving emission inventories and air quality assessments in China. Satellite observations, such as those of formaldehyde and glyoxal from GOME-2A and OMI, have been employed to provide quantitative constraints on non-methane volatile organic compound (NMVOC) emissions, refining spatial estimates and enhancing the differentiation of emission precursors [chunk_000330; chunk_000452; chunk_004665]. Complementary bottom-up approaches, such as the use of frameworks like MEIC and MIX, allow for detailed emissions mapping using empirical spatial proxies and high-resolution datasets, albeit with inherent uncertainties tied to human activity data, proxy reliability, and regional differences [chunk_002820; chunk_002883; chunk_003109]. Combining satellite-based inversion experiments with ground-based inventories addresses these limitations by aligning global observational data with localized emission profiles, fostering a more accurate understanding of pollutant distribution and facilitating targeted air quality interventions [chunk_003013; chunk_003994; chunk_002994].

sections.step_name: Spatial and Temporal Analysis of Vehicle Emissions
sections.response: The spatial and temporal analysis of vehicle emissions reveals significant heterogeneity influenced by factors such as emission standards, vehicle types, and driving patterns. In China, light-duty gasoline vehicles (LDGVs) constitute over 90% of the vehicle fleet and have experienced rapid annual growth, making their accurate emission estimation critical for air quality management and public health considerations [chunk_003535][chunk_003658]. Studies leveraging advanced inventories, such as the MEIC model, and big data, including high-resolution emission profiles, have provided detailed insights into pollutant variations across different emission standards and operational conditions [chunk_003013][chunk_003638]. Traffic congestion substantially impacts emissions, with higher concentrations observed during low-speed conditions, underscoring the importance of traffic flow management in mitigating urban air pollution [chunk_004630]. Additionally, refined methodologies, such as using road networks for emissions allocation, have improved regional inventories and the interpretation of spatial emission disparities [chunk_002828]. To address these challenges, systematic approaches identifying high-emission vehicles and reinforcing emission standards are essential for future urban and environmental planning [chunk_003646][chunk_003769].

sections.step_name: Evaluation of China's Air Clean Plan
sections.response: China's Air Clean Plan (ACP), implemented in 2013, significantly contributed to reducing ambient PM2.5 concentrations and associated health risks. Over the first phase of the program (2014–2017), PM2.5 concentrations decreased by an average of 33.65%, with continued improvements observed between 2018 and 2020, resulting in a substantial reduction in PM2.5-induced premature deaths and exposure levels across the country [chunk_002586; chunk_002630; chunk_004579]. Regions such as Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei (BTH) reported the most drastic improvements, though many areas remain above WHO air quality standards [chunk_004575; chunk_004577]. The program achieved co-benefits by integrating air pollution control and carbon mitigation strategies, which reduced environmental and health burdens [chunk_002541; chunk_003520]. However, challenges persist in maintaining long-term reductions, addressing regional disparities, and fully integrating economic growth with ecological sustainability [chunk_004579; chunk_003467]. These findings underscore the necessity of continued efforts, tailored regional strategies, and enhanced emission control policies to sustain and expand ACP's impact.

Multi-objective mitigation strategies, such as China's Air Clean Plan (ACP) implemented since 2013, have demonstrated significant environmental and health benefits. The ACP, comprising the Air Pollution Prevention and Control Action Plan (2013-2017) and the Three-Year Action Plan (2018-2020), achieved notable reductions in PM2.5 concentrations and related premature deaths nationwide, especially in regions like Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei (BTH) and the Yangtze River Delta (YRD) [chunk_002541; chunk_004579; chunk_004577]. These measures contributed to a 33.65% decline in average PM2.5 levels between 2014 and 2020 [chunk_002586]. The integration of air pollution control with carbon emissions mitigation further amplified co-benefits, offsetting the rise in premature deaths driven by population and economic growth during the ACP's first phase [chunk_002629; chunk_002636]. However, challenges such as the need for geographically refined strategies and the decoupling of economic growth from emissions persist, underscoring the necessity for continued efforts to maximize synergistic environmental and climate outcomes [chunk_003525; chunk_002637].

sections.step_name: Health Outcomes Tied to PM2.5 Exposure
sections.response: Exposure to fine particulate matter (PM2.5) has been linked to significant health risks, including increased premature mortality from diseases such as ischemic heart disease, stroke, lung cancer, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, accounting for substantial proportions of disease burden in China [chunk_002542; chunk_002598]. China implemented the Air Clean Plan (ACP) in 2013 to combat these health risks, achieving notable reductions in PM2.5 concentrations and related premature deaths between 2014 and 2020 [chunk_002541; chunk_004579]. Specifically, measures under the ACP avoided approximately 0.10 million premature deaths from 2014 to 2017 by addressing both PM2.5 exposure levels and regional vulnerabilities [chunk_002629; chunk_004577]. Despite these improvements, certain disease-specific mortality rates, like from lung cancer, did not exhibit reductions, underscoring the persistent health challenges associated with PM2.5 [chunk_002625]. The success of the ACP highlights the need for sustained and targeted regional interventions to further mitigate PM2.5-related health outcomes [chunk_004580].

Uncertainty plays a significant role in health impact assessments, particularly when estimating emissions and their effects on air quality and health. Bottom-up emission inventories, which rely on empirical spatial proxies, often introduce uncertainties due to incomplete knowledge of human activities, emission factors, and source distributions. These uncertainties can affect the spatial and temporal representation of emissions in models, leading to discrepancies between modeled and observed data [chunk_002885; chunk_002888]. Monte Carlo simulations have been widely used to assess these uncertainties, providing probabilistic estimates and narrowing error margins, although inconsistencies remain when compared with satellite observations [chunk_003896; chunk_003783]. Regional and national studies in China have highlighted the variability in uncertainties across provincial and local scales, demonstrating the complexities introduced by differing spatial and economic factors [chunk_003900; chunk_003908]. Such insights emphasize the need for improved methodologies and refined data in emission inventories to enhance the reliability of health impact assessments.

synthesis.response:
### Core Hypothesis
Accurately assessing air pollution sources, mitigation effectiveness, and environmental health impacts in China necessitates integrating refined emission inventories, advanced observational techniques, and multi-objective mitigation strategies. This approach must address uncertainties in emission data, spatial-temporal resolutions, and health impact models while promoting regionalized control measures and long-term ecological sustainability.

### Evidence Analysis
1. **Emission Inventories and Uncertainties**: Bottom-up emission inventories form the backbone of air pollution assessments but are hampered by significant uncertainties related to human activity data and empirical spatial proxies. In China, models such as MEIC and MIX have leveraged high-resolution data to enhance spatial accuracy, though discrepancies persist due to regional heterogeneity and incomplete harmonization [chunk_002820; chunk_002985; chunk_002649]. Advances integrating satellite constraints, such as those from OMI and GOME-2A observations, have improved emission estimates, better aligning modeled distributions with real-world air quality patterns [chunk_004676; chunk_000330]. However, challenges in quantifying uncertainties, particularly at provincial and urban scales, complicate efforts to create universally accurate inventories [chunk_002987; chunk_003896].

2. **Integration of Observational Techniques**: Combining satellite-based inversion techniques with ground-based measurements has emerged as a vital method, enhancing emission profile differentiation and pollutant distribution modeling [chunk_003013; chunk_002994]. The use of bottom-up frameworks like MEIC alongside high-resolution satellite observations offers holistic insights into emission sources, particularly for volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and vehicle emissions [chunk_002883; chunk_002820]. These integration efforts reveal spatial and temporal emission patterns critical for targeted mitigation strategies, though uncertainties related to satellite calibration and ground proxy validity remain [chunk_003109; chunk_002994].

3. **Mitigation Strategies and Effectiveness**: China's Air Clean Plan (ACP) exemplifies a multi-objective strategy that has significantly reduced PM2.5 concentrations nationwide, achieving a 33.65% decline from 2014 to 2020 and avoiding approximately 0.10 million premature deaths [chunk_002586; chunk_002541; chunk_004579]. The integration of air pollution control with carbon mitigation produced synergistic environmental outcomes, particularly in regions like Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei (BTH) and the Yangtze River Delta (YRD) [chunk_002541; chunk_004577]. However, the persistence of challenges such as regional disparities and the coupling of economic growth with emissions underscores the necessity for geographically refined interventions and enhanced emission control policies [chunk_003467; chunk_002637].

4. **Health Impacts of Air Pollution**: PM2.5 exposure contributes to significant health risks, including ischemic heart disease, lung cancer, and stroke, underscoring the importance of mitigation programs like the ACP [chunk_002542; chunk_002598]. Despite reductions in PM2.5-induced premature deaths between 2014 and 2020 due to ACP measures, persistent disease-specific mortality rates highlight the need for sustained and targeted interventions [chunk_002625; chunk_004580]. Health impact assessments remain hindered by uncertainties in emission inventories, where inconsistencies between models and observed data necessitate refined methodologies, such as Monte Carlo simulations for probabilistic estimates [chunk_003783; chunk_003896].

5. **Vehicle Emissions Analysis**: Vehicle emissions represent a critical air pollution source in urban China, with over 90% of the fleet consisting of light-duty gasoline vehicles (LDGVs) [chunk_003535]. Advanced emission profiling through the MEIC model and data derived from road networks has improved spatial emission allocation, revealing high rates of pollution during traffic congestion [chunk_002828; chunk_003638]. Mitigation measures targeting high-emission vehicles and enforcing stricter emission standards provide actionable pathways for reducing urban air pollution [chunk_003646; chunk_004630].

### Critical Gaps
1. **Data Harmonization Across Scales**: The lack of harmonization in emission inventory formats, geographic scales, and temporal resolutions hinders inter-regional and global comparability, affecting policy consistency [chunk_002649; chunk_002987].
2. **Uncertainty Quantification**: There is a persistent need for better methodologies to quantify and narrow uncertainties in both bottom-up and satellite-based inventories, especially regarding human activity factors and calibration tools [chunk_003896; chunk_003783].
3. **Health Impact Modeling**: Current health impact assessments often fail to fully integrate variability across spatial scales and multi-factorial predictors, limiting their reliability [chunk_003900; chunk_002885].
4. **Regional Disparities in Mitigation Effectiveness**: The success of the ACP has been uneven across regions, with many areas still exceeding WHO air quality standards. Developing region-specific strategies is essential for equitable environmental health benefits [chunk_004575; chunk_003467].
5. **Sustainable Integration of Mitigation and Economic Growth**: Long-term sustainability of air pollution reductions is challenged by the coupling of emissions with economic growth, highlighting the importance of strategies promoting low-carbon development [chunk_002637; chunk_004579].

### Conclusion
Addressing air pollution in China requires an integrative approach that synthesizes emission inventories, observational data, mitigation strategies, and health impact assessments. Efforts like China's Air Clean Plan demonstrate the potential for significant environmental and health improvements, yet critical gaps remain in data accuracy, regional equity, and sustainable development. Refining emission inventories via harmonization, reducing uncertainties through advanced modeling techniques, and implementing geographically tailored mitigation measures can significantly enhance the effectiveness of air pollution control and improve public health outcomes. A focus on decoupling economic growth from emissions will be key to ensuring long-term environmental sustainability.
